title: AI Adoption Through Forward-Deployed Engineers - LinkedIn
full_text:
Through my work with Kela Technologies, Poolside, Conntour and other companies that build AI for classified, high-stakes defense and intelligence environments, I've come to learn and appreciate the role of the forward-deployed engineer (FDE).
Through my work with Kela Technologies, Poolside, Conntour and other companies that build AI for classified, high-stakes defense and intelligence environments, I've come to learn and appreciate the role of the forward-deployed engineer (FDE).
These are probably the hardest places in the world to deploy AI: air-gapped, immune to hype, and nothing gets adopted because a vendor said so.
What gets AI adopted there is a role: the forward-deployed engineer. An engineer who joins the customer's team, works inside their environment, and stays until the system is part of how the unit actually operates.
Working alongside these companies as a communications advisor has taught me more about how AI enters an organization than any product launch I've been part of. The lessons apply, I think, to every company now embedding AI in its work:
🚶🏼‍➡️ AI gets adopted through people, not products. The license is the start. Until someone trusted is inside the workflow making the system useful every day, nothing has been deployed, only installed.
🔐 Adoption is measured by your absence. The deployment has succeeded when the customer's team runs the system without the vendor in the room. A team that can't isn't a success story, it's a dependency. If the customer chooses to keep the FDE around, it's for their trusted judgment, and because they're already building the next thing.
🗑️ What you decline to automate is a decision not a gap. The disciplined deployments come down to where the system is genuinely trustworthy. The undisciplined ones automate broadly and get quietly shelved.
🛠️ The people who can do this are hybrids: technically deep, skilled in their craft, fluent in the customer's world, and comfortable being accountable for outcomes. This is what Hostinger hires for, people who constantly develop "two dominant hands", being able to perform but also being able to transform.
And one lesson for my own field: as AI changes how companies work, comms people have to deploy forward too. You can't tell the story of work you've only seen in a demo deck. Sit inside the team. Learn the system. Actually use your client's products. Earn the right to speak for them.
Still learning all of this. Happy to compare notes with anyone working the same systems.
